Piker said politicians, like Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., risk appearing uncommitted to their positions when they sound hesitant or defensive.

"If you come across as, like, even a little worried or even a little defensive, then people respond to that with, ‘Oh, maybe she doesn’t believe this either,’" Piker said.

AOC SLAMS ‘OPPRESSED’ TRUMP STATES WHILE TOUTING NYC SERVICES DESPITE $5B DEFICIT AND TRANSIT CRIME SPIKE

The Twitch streamer continued that line of criticism of Ocasio-Cortez, saying she's embraced ambitious policies but sometimes focuses too heavily on public perception.

"The way she carries herself, I think, is a little bit too, like, optics-driven, too liberal and too optics-driven," Piker said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani and Ocasio-Cortez for comment, but did not immediately hear back.

FLASHBACK: MAMDANI'S HISTORY OF DODGING QUESTIONS ABOUT WHERE HE STANDS ON KEY ISSUES

Piker contrasted Ocasio-Cortez with Mamdani, whom he praised for combining progressive policies with language capable of reaching liberal and less traditional audiences.

"I think Zohran has done a pretty good job of merging those two worlds where he speaks the language of liberalism," Piker said.

Hasan asked whether Piker wanted the interview to end with him telling one of his political allies to "shut the f--- up." Piker stressed that his frustration did not represent a break with Mamdani.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"I mean, come on," Piker said. "I love the guy. I have my disagreements with him from time to time."

Piker later accepted Hasan’s revised description of the approach he wanted from progressive politicians.

"Bold, brave and unapologetic," Piker said.

item.title: Liberty's Sabrina Ionescu addresses absence from WNBA 3-point contest: 'Never officially got invited'
item.description: Sabrina Ionescu says she was never officially invited to the WNBA 3-point contest, contradicting reports she declined to participate in All-Star weekend.
item.content:encoded:
The lineup for this year’s WNBA All-Star 3-point contest has been a talking point in the sports world since it was announced. 

New York Liberty guard Sabrina Ionescu was among the most notable players absent from the annual competition.

Ionescu addressed the speculation ahead of Wednesday’s Liberty-Sky matchup, pushing back against reports that she chose not to participate in this weekend’s WNBA 3-point contest in Chicago. 

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"I can't decline an offer I never got officially invited to. So, was pretty surprised to see that. Never got invited," the defending 3-point contest champion told reporters pregame.

Caitlin Clark and Kayla McBride were also among the high-profile stars who reportedly declined invitations to the competition.

"Whether it's against Steph Curry or in the W, I always go out and try and beat my record, every single time I've gone out," said Ionescu, who has won the 3-point contest twice, highlighted by her record-setting 37-point performance in 2023.

"I just kind of wanted to set the record straight," she continued. "Like that was just false, but I'm really excited for the participants in it, and obviously there's going to be a new winner. So, I'm excited to see who wins."

Ionescu later referenced her previous 3-point showdown with Stephen Curry when asked a follow-up about the issue.

"Maybe I'll do it with Steph again at NBA All-Star and get a really nice official invite," she said. 

The confirmed list of 3-point contest participants includes Dallas' Azzi Fudd, Atlanta's Rhyne Howard, Golden State's Janelle Salaün, Portland's Bridget Carleton, Toronto's Marina Mabrey and Seattle's Natisha Hiedeman. Howard and Mabrey are the only players in that group with prior 3-point contest experience at All-Star weekend.

Earlier this week, Ionescu refuted a report that she had opted out of this year’s festivities, commenting "lies" under a since-deleted Bleacher Report Instagram post. McBride also weighed in via social media, saying her agent had texted her about the event in the 48 hours before her Instagram story post, but no formal invitation ever came.

"Posting things like I'm afraid of shooting smoke is weird and if you didn't hear it from me prob shouldn't believe it," McBride wrote. "We should focus on the great shooters in it already!!! Because there's some killas in there."

Despite dealing with an ankle injury early this season, Ionescu entered Wednesday night’s game averaging 13.8 points in 14 games.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Bear caught on camera perched atop New Mexico utility pole before reportedly suffering fatal electrocution
item.description: Viral video shows a bear perched atop a utility pole along a rural New Mexico highway, panting and struggling to keep its balance on the precarious perch.
item.content:encoded:
A bear that climbed to the top of a utility pole in rural New Mexico became the subject of a viral video this week before reportedly later dying after the unusual encounter.

The video, recorded Monday near Gladstone, New Mexico, roughly 170 miles east of Santa Fe, shows the bear precariously perched on the crossarm of the utility pole while panting and trying to maintain its balance high above the ground.

The U.S. Department of the Interior shared the video Wednesday, joking, "We appreciate the enthusiasm for American Energy Dominance, but this isn't what we meant."

WILDLIFE PHOTOGRAPHER STAYS ICE-COLD AS MASSIVE BROWN BEAR WALKS RIGHT UP TO HIS CAMERA

The Department of the Interior and the New Mexico Department of Wildlife did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital’s request for comment.

The footage, verified by Storyful, also includes audio from a 911 caller reporting the unusual sight.

"I'm calling to report a bear on top of a light pole," the caller tells a dispatcher.

WOMAN STALKED BY CHARGING BEAR ON MORNING DOG WALK CAPTURES TERRIFYING ENCOUNTER ON CAMERA

The dispatcher responded that state officials had already been notified but said tranquilizing the animal while it remained atop the pole could have caused it to fall.

Shannon Mullens, who recorded the video, told Storyful she was traveling from Red River, New Mexico, to Oklahoma on Highway 56 when she and her family spotted what they initially believed was a dead bear.

"We really didn't believe what we saw at first, so we turned around to get another look," Mullens said.

HIDDEN DANGER AT POPULAR US NATIONAL PARK FORCES TRAIL CLOSURES AS BEAR SIGHTINGS CLIMB

"At first, we thought that the bear was dead, until we got closer to it, and then we realized that he was still alive."

The bear was electrocuted and died before wildlife officials could safely rescue it, the New York Post reported.

Wildlife experts say bears can climb utility poles after becoming startled by people, traffic or dogs, treating them much like trees while trying to escape danger.

A similar incident ended differently in Arizona in 2021, when wildlife officials safely rescued a bear that had climbed a utility pole.

item.title: Teachers who once dated and share child arrested after classroom argument spiraled into violence: police
item.description: Both teachers at The SEED School of Miami face domestic violence battery charges after an alleged assault and pepper spray attack on school property.
item.content:encoded:
A personal dispute between two teachers who were formerly a couple erupted inside a Miami charter boarding school, ending with both under arrest after investigators say a classroom meeting turned violent.

According to a Miami-Dade Schools Police arrest affidavit obtained by Fox News Digital, Terrod Tiyan Torrence, 36, and Odiria Biggins were previously involved in a romantic relationship and share a child together.

A few days before the confrontation, Biggins told investigators Torrence left a handwritten note on her desk asking if they could talk.

She agreed to meet him July 17 inside Classroom 119 at The SEED School of Miami, the affidavit said.

SJSU VOLLEYBALL COACH'S ALLEGED ASSAULT VICTIM SPEAKS OUT FOR THE FIRST TIME SINCE ALLEGATIONS SURFACED

What began as a conversation quickly turned physical, according to investigators.

Biggins told police Torrence repeatedly punched her in the left side of the head before slamming her head into a wall, causing her to lose consciousness and collapse to the floor.

MARCELLUS WILEY'S WIFE CLAIMS EX-NFL STAR THREATENED TO KILL HER BEFORE FOURTH OF JULY DOMESTIC BATTERY ARREST

But the confrontation didn't end there, investigators said.

After regaining consciousness, Biggins allegedly walked to her vehicle, grabbed a canister of pepper spray and later tracked Torrence down elsewhere on campus before spraying him, according to Miami ABC affiliate WSVN.

Surveillance video captured Biggins entering a classroom where Torrence was, the outlet reported.

BRING THE BEAR SPRAY TO THE WEDDING — YOU NEVER KNOW WHEN YOU NEED TO USE IT ON A FAMILY MEMBER

Moments later, both emerged with Torrence appearing to struggle walking and suffering visible eye irritation before school staff rushed to help him. He was later taken to a hospital for treatment.

Biggins was arrested Monday and charged with discharging a weapon on school property for allegedly using pepper spray on campus, along with misdemeanor domestic violence battery, according to WSVN. A judge found probable cause and set her bond at $2,000.

Torrence was arrested Tuesday by Miami-Dade Schools Police and charged with misdemeanor domestic violence battery.

Court records show he was booked into the Turner Guilford Knight Correctional Center before appearing before Judge Alicia Garcia Priovolos, who imposed a $2,000 standard bond and a pretrial no-contact order barring him from contacting Biggins. His arraignment is scheduled for Sept. 23.

It remains unclear what sparked the argument or whether any students witnessed the confrontation.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Miami-Dade Schools Police Department and The SEED School of Miami for additional information, including whether either teacher remains employed.

item.title: Sophie Cunningham doubles down ahead of Sun game after ESPN profile stirs controversy: 'I said what I said'
item.description: Sophie Cunningham told WNBA reporters she refuses to back down on protecting women's sports, citing Title IX and her belief in safeguarding girls.
item.content:encoded:
Before Wednesday's game against the Connecticut Sun, Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham doubled down on her call to protect women's sports from biological male competitors, brushing aside recent backlash over her comments.

"I think I said what I said. I think it's kind of common sense," Cunningham told reporters when asked to expand on her views.

WATCH (via @RomeovilleKid / X):

"I'll always believe in that. It's really important to protect children, and that's little girls who are also involved in that category."

SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM MANAGES TO ANGER BOTH THE LEFT AND RIGHT WITH A SIMPLE SOCIAL MEDIA POST

For Cunningham, defending women's sports isn't about politics. It's about protecting female athletes.

"I'm not a very political person, but I do have beliefs. I have morals, and I'll always stand on those," she explained.

She pointed to Title IX as the foundation of women's sports, arguing the law exists to protect opportunities for female athletes. Without those protections, she said, the category loses its purpose.

TRANS ATHLETES DROP LAWSUIT TO GAIN ACCESS TO GIRLS' SPORTS IN NEW HAMPSHIRE AFTER SCOTUS RULING

Cunningham also rejected the idea that her position stems from bigotry, saying defending women's sports doesn't mean harboring hatred toward anyone else.

"I extend love, and I don't dislike anyone. I think there is room for absolutely everyone here," she said, adding that she has never expressed hatred toward the trans community.

Her focus, she said, remains on protecting biological women and girls.

TRACK STAR SAYS SHE'S TIRED OF CHRISTIANS BEING CALLED 'BIGOTS' FOR OPPOSING TRANS ATHLETES IN GIRLS' SPORTS

OutKick founder Clay Travis shared the clip on X.

"Love all of this. Well done @sophaller," Travis wrote in part.

He later tagged author J.K. Rowling, writing, "Hey @jk_rowling, want to make sure you see this. Sanity is winning in America."

As for the backlash, Cunningham said she tunes it out.

"I really don't care," she said regarding the political labels attached to her views. "If I'm gonna live by the love, I'll die by the hate, and that's just not who I am. I beat to my own drum."

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

item.title: Jack Smith hit with criminal referral as GOP moves testimony fight toward prosecution
item.description: Jim Jordan issued a criminal referral against Jack Smith, accusing him of making false statements to Congress about accessing lawmakers' text messages.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Former Biden special counsel Jack Smith could face potential criminal prosecution after Republicans accused the ex-prosecutor of misleading Congress about the scope of his investigation into President Donald Trump.

House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan, R-Ohio, issued a criminal referral for Smith Wednesday, arguing newly released records contradict Smith's testimony to his committee that he did not obtain or review lawmakers' text messages during his multiple probes into President Donald Trump.

"All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different," Jordan wrote in a letter to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Wednesday. "Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee’s oversight.

"His conduct can only be understood as an effort to thwart the Committee’s inquiry by intentionally making false statements and representations before Congress," Jordan continued, adding that the Justice Department should examine whether Smith's actions warrant criminal charges.

VETERAN DEM BREAKS PARTY LINE AS JACK SMITH SPYING SCANDAL GRIPS CAPITOL HILL

Jordan’s letter accused Smith of making false statements during a December deposition before the House Judiciary Committee. Specifically, Jordan argued that "responses provided by Mr. Smith gave the impression that neither Mr. Smith nor his team had access to the content of members’ privileged text messages."

"We now know this to be false," the letter adds.

The referral comes after Smith faced mounting scrutiny from Republicans over allegedly weaponizing the Justice Department and violating constitutional privacy protections during his investigations into alleged interference during the 2020 election and Trump’s handling of classified documents.

He has denied any wrongdoing.

And Rep. Jamie Raskin, D-Md., the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, blasted Jordan's referral. 

"This referral deliberately distorts and misstates the facts, including what questions the Republicans actually asked at Mr. Smith's interview. The Republicans fault Mr. Smith for not reading the minds of congressional investigators and for not volunteering information they didn't ask for. This is absurd. Mr. Smith may be telegenic but he is not telepathic," Raskin said in a statement.

Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley, R-Iowa, released documents last week showing Smith's team read the text messages of 44 members of Congress from both parties and White House staff during its investigation.

Targeted lawmakers included Jordan and Grassley in addition to the late Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C.; Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine; House Majority Leader Steve Scalise, R-La.; and Sen. Cory Booker, D-N.J.

Trump officials affected included former Vice President Mike Pence, White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller, FBI Director Kash Patel and Ivanka Trump, among others in the president's orbit during his first term.

BIDEN SPECIAL COUNSEL’S 'RUNAWAY TRAIN’ SCOOPED UP SENSITIVE LAWMAKER INFO: 'ABUSE OF POWER'

Grassley's office also found that Smith's team appeared to violate investigative protocols by accessing text messages that had not been reviewed by a "filter team," a safeguard designed to prevent investigators from accessing privileged information, according to the documents.

When Smith was asked by congressional investigators if he examined the contents of lawmakers’ text messages during his probe, he said, "no."

He told the House Judiciary Committee his team only sought telephone toll records.

"Toll records were sought for historical telephone routing information, collected after calls had taken place, identifying the incoming and outgoing call numbers, the time of the calls and their duration," Smith testified in December. "Toll records do not include the content of calls."

The Justice Department told Fox News Digital when reached for comment, "The Department can confirm receipt of the criminal referral from the House Judiciary Committee and will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct."

item.title: Trump salutes 'great heroes' as he attends dignified transfer of US service members killed in Jordan, Iraq
item.description: President Donald Trump said ahead of a dignified transfer ceremony that the four U.S. service members killed in action overseas were "great heroes."
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump on Wednesday saluted the flag-draped caskets of four U.S. service members recently killed in action in Jordan and Iraq while attended a dignified transfer ceremony at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware. 

Trump stood alongside Secretary of War Pete Hegseth as they paid tribute to 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii; Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York; and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, who were killed in Jordan, and Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, from Fayetteville, North Carolina, who was killed in Iraq. 

Trump told reporters ahead of the ceremony the service members were "great heroes." 

"We're going to honor them. And it’s one of the hardest things to do — for me, it's one of the hardest things to do as a president. But it has to be done," Trump added. 

US SOLDIER KILLED IN JORDAN ATTACK REMEMBERED AS LIVING ‘LIFE OF COURAGE AND SELFLESSNESS’ 

"All I'm going to say is we love you. We love your child," Trump said when asked if he had a message for the families. "And that's what they are to them. They're their children. There's no games, no nothing. That's their child. And all you could do is throw out your heart." 

Feehan’s parents, Stephen and Shari, told KUTV Tuesday, "No parents could have asked for a better son, and carrying your legacy forward is now our purpose.

"To that end, we're building a scholarship in his name — one meant to open doors to education for those who serve or hope to. We're starting it with his death gratuity, and we welcome anyone moved to give." 

RUBIO ACCUSES IRAN OF FLYING IRGC PERSONNEL INTO YEMEN AS HOUTHI THREAT ESCALATES 

U.S. Central Command said over the weekend that the service members were killed at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan as CENTCOM and partner forces "defended against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks." 

Swinton was killed while troops attempted to destroy a downed Iranian attack drone Sunday at an air base in Iraq. 

Both incidents remain under investigation. 

Fox News’ Eric Mack and Jennifer Griffin contributed to this report.

item.title: Survivor of Hamas terror attack in Israel slams Mamdani over 'dangerous' video about Netanyahu
item.description: Mazal Tazazo said a video NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani released calling Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" is "dangerous."
item.content:encoded:
A survivor of the Nova music festival terrorist attack by Hamas pushed back against a video released by Zohran Mamdani calling Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal," saying the New York City mayor’s comments were "dangerous." 

Mazal Tazazo made the remark in a video shared on X by radio host Jason Rantz, one day after Mamdani said Netanyahu was "the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people."

In Mamdani’s message, he said he agreed with the International Criminal Court that "Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes" and urged the federal government to carry out an arrest warrant if Netanyahu visits New York City in September for the U.N. General Assembly. 

MAMDANI GOES BACK ON CAMPAIGN PROMISE, SAYS NYC CAN’T ARREST NETANYAHU 

"I was shocked today when I saw that video. It's so dangerous," Tazazo said.  

"What's happening there is not genocide. People died. Yes, this is a war. It's very sad. But why? Because the terrorists hide behind their people," she continued, referencing statements from the Israel Defense Forces that Hamas uses civilian buildings as cover for their military activity. 

"If you want to really want to understand what's going on here, so come," she added. "I'm Mazal Tazazo, nice to meet you, survivor of the Nova. You can come to Israel, nobody gonna arrest you, OK? You can come to Israel and hear my story and hear the story of Israel and go around the place." 

Rantz wrote on X that Tazazo "absolutely unloaded on Zohran Mamdani for threatening to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu." 

NEW YORK DEMOCRAT HOPES MAMDANI WON’T BE ‘ARRESTING ANYONE’ AFTER RECENT NETANYAHU COMMENTS 

Mamdani said in his own video posted on X Tuesday that "my administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here.

"It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.

"He is responsible for the killing of more than 73,000 people, for the maiming of tens of thousands of children, as those who survive undergo amputation without anesthesia, for the targeting of neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers, denying newborns even the chance to live, for the countless people he starved as he blocked food and humanitarian aid from reaching them," Mamdani also claimed about Netanyahu.
